questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of firm, painless swellings on her Achilles tendons. She has no other symptoms. Her total cholesterol is 7.2 mmol/L, LDL cholesterol 5.3 mmol/L, HDL 1.2 mmol/L, and triglycerides 1.6 mmol/L. Her father had a myocardial infarction at age 45. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Tendon xanthomas with a high LDL and strong family history of premature ASCVD are classic for familial hypercholesterolaemia. Sitosterolaemia also causes tendon xanthomas but typically has normal or only mildly elevated LDL and elevated plant sterols. Polygenic hypercholesterolaemia lacks xanthomas, and familial combined hyperlipidaemia usually presents with mixed dyslipidaemia.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old asymptomatic man attends a routine health check. He is a non-smoker with a normal BMI and no known medical conditions. Blood tests show total cholesterol 6.8 mmol/L, LDL 4.4 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, and triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L. No tendon xanthomas are present. His father died of a heart attack at age 72, and his mother is alive at 75 with hypertension. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Polygenic hyperlipidaemia is the most common cause of elevated cholesterol in this age group and typically presents with moderate elevation of LDL without xanthomas or strong family history. Familial combined hyperlipidaemia often features high triglycerides and a stronger family history of early CAD. Familial hypercholesterolaemia would usually have tendon xanthomas or very high LDL (>5 mmol/L).

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. He has no history of gallstones or alcohol excess. Serum amylase is 950 U/L, and venous blood is lipemic. Fasting lipid profile shows triglycerides 42 mmol/L and total cholesterol 8.1 mmol/L. He does not have xanthomas. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This presentation of acute pancreatitis with severe hypertriglyceridaemia (>40 mmol/L) in a young patient without secondary causes is most likely due to familial hypertriglyceridaemia (type V). Familial combined hyperlipidaemia usually has moderate hypertriglyceridaemia (<10 mmol/L). Dysbetalipoproteinaemia is associated with both high cholesterol and triglycerides, but typically less extreme.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is referred to the lipid clinic after his father died suddenly from a myocardial infarction at age 36. The child has multiple raised yellow-orange nodules over his knees and elbows. Fasting lipid profile shows total cholesterol 7.5 mmol/L, LDL 5.8 mmol/L, HDL 1.1 mmol/L, and triglycerides 1.4 mmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary hyperlipidaemia due to nephrotic syndrome
questions.explanation: In a child with tuberous xanthomas, very high LDL, and a strong family history of premature CAD, heterozygous FH is the most likely diagnosis. Homozygous FH would present with much higher LDL (>13 mmol/L) and xanthomas earlier in life. Sitosterolaemia causes xanthomas but with normal or mildly elevated LDL, and polygenic hypercholesterolaemia does not cause xanthomas in childhood.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of hoarseness. Laryngoscopy reveals a yellowish plaque on her left vocal cord. She has no other symptoms. Fasting lipid profile shows total cholesterol 6.1 mmol/L, LDL 4.2 mmol/L, HDL 1.3 mmol/L, and triglycerides 1.8 mmol/L. Her mother had a coronary angioplasty at age 50. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Xanthomas at an unusual site (vocal cord) with moderately elevated LDL and a family history of early CAD point to heterozygous FH. Sitosterolaemia can also cause xanthomas but is rare and usually presents with normolipidaemia. Familial defective ApoB-100 is a variant of FH but is less common; FH is the most likely overall.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with bilateral Achilles tendon xanthomata. She has a strong family history of premature coronary artery disease. Her fasting lipid profile shows total cholesterol 8.2 mmol/L, LDL 6.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.2 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.8 mmol/L. According to NICE guidelines, what is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of familial hypercholesterolaemia?
options: DNA analysis for LDL receptor mutation
questions.explanation: NICE recommends genetic testing (DNA analysis) to confirm familial hypercholesterolaemia when clinical criteria (e.g., Simon Broome) suggest a high probability. The patient's xanthomata, high LDL, and family history make a clinical diagnosis probable; genetic testing is the definitive next step.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is found to have a fasting total cholesterol of 7.5 mmol/L on routine health screen. He has no family history of hyperlipidaemia or premature cardiovascular disease. He feels well. His BMI is 32 kg/m². Which single investigation is most likely to identify a secondary cause for his hyperlipidaemia?
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism is a common reversible cause of hypercholesterolaemia due to reduced LDL receptor expression. NICE guidelines recommend checking TSH in all patients with new hyperlipidaemia before labelling it as primary.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man is admitted with acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. His lipid profile taken 12 hours after symptom onset shows: total cholesterol 4.0 mmol/L, LDL 2.2 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.5 mmol/L. He has no prior history of hyperlipidaemia. Which of the following best interprets these results?
options: The HDL is low due to the infarction
His lipid profile is reassuring and indicates no need for statin therapy
His LDL is accurately measured and reflects his usual level
The LDL is falsely lowered due to acute phase response and should be repeated in 4-6 weeks
questions.explanation: Acute illness, including MI, lowers total cholesterol and LDL via the acute phase response. Therefore, the measured LDL is an underestimate. Repeat lipid profile in 4-6 weeks is recommended for accurate assessment.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with no symptoms but a family history of premature coronary disease (father had MI at age 45) is referred for lipid assessment. His fasting LDL is 5.0 mmol/L. He has no tendon xanthomata or arcus cornealis. According to the Simon Broome criteria, what is the most appropriate classification for his possible familial hypercholesterolaemia?
questions.explanation: Simon Broome criteria: LDL >4.9 meets cholesterol criterion. Patient lacks tendon xanthomata, so not definite. He has a first-degree relative with premature CAD (<60), which fulfills family history criterion for probable FH.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has a fasting lipid profile: total cholesterol 5.2 mmol/L, HDL 1.1 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.8 mmol/L. Her calculated LDL is 1.9 mmol/L (using Friedewald equation). However, given her triglyceride level, which of the following is the most accurate measure of her cardiovascular risk from lipids?
questions.explanation: With triglycerides >2.3 mmol/L, the Friedewald equation underestimates LDL. Non-HDL cholesterol (total – HDL) includes all atherogenic particles and is reliable in hypertriglyceridaemia. NICE recommends non-HDL for risk assessment in such cases.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with xanthelasma and a family history of premature coronary disease has a total cholesterol of 9.2 mmol/L. He is diagnosed with familial hypercholesterolaemia. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 20 mg or rosuvastatin 10 mg) as first-line for FH. Atorvastatin 20 mg is the typical starting dose; rosuvastatin 40 mg is too high for initial therapy. Simvastatin is moderate-intensity, ezetimibe and fenofibrate are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and mixed hyperlipidaemia (LDL 3.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 4.5 mmol/L) has been on atorvastatin 20 mg for 6 months but developed myalgia with normal CK. She wants to continue lipid-lowering therapy. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In statin intolerance, NICE recommends ezetimibe monotherapy as an alternative. Switching to another statin may also be considered but myalgia often recurs; ezetimibe is the safer choice. Adding ezetimibe while continuing atorvastatin would not address the intolerance.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with acute epigastric pain radiating to the back. He has no gallstones or alcohol excess. His triglyceride level is 18 mmol/L. CT confirms acute pancreatitis. What is the most appropriate long-term lipid-lowering therapy to prevent recurrence?
questions.explanation: Severe hypertriglyceridaemia (>10 mmol/L) causing pancreatitis requires fibrate therapy (e.g., fenofibrate) as first-line to reduce triglyceride levels and prevent recurrence. Statins are less effective for triglycerides; omega-3 is second-line; insulin is for acute management of DKA, not long-term prevention.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known heterozygous FH and previous myocardial infarction is on atorvastatin 80 mg and ezetimibe 10 mg. His LDL cholesterol remains 4.5 mmol/L despite adherence. What is the most appropriate next therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a PCSK9 inhibitor (evolocumab or alirocumab) for FH patients with established CVD and LDL >4.0 mmol/L despite maximal statin and ezetimibe. Fenofibrate is not indicated, rosuvastatin 40 mg is not more effective than atorvastatin 80 mg, colesevelam is less potent, and atorvastatin 120 mg is off-label and unsafe.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with FH, treated with atorvastatin 20 mg, is now 8 weeks pregnant. She asks about her cholesterol treatment. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy. If lipid-lowering therapy is required (e.g., very high LDL), a bile acid sequestrant like cholestyramine is the safest option as it is not systemically absorbed. Ezetimibe and fenofibrate are not recommended in pregnancy. Simply discontinuing all therapy may be acceptable if LDL is not extremely high, but the question asks for 'most appropriate' therapy; given she was on treatment, cholestyramine is the preferred active option.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old asymptomatic man presents to the GP after a routine health check. His total cholesterol is 6.8 mmol/L, HDL 1.2 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.8 mmol/L. He is a non-smoker, BMI 28, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg. He has no personal or family history of CVD. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Advise lifestyle modification alone and review in 6 months
Refer to the lipid clinic for further testing
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend using QRISK3 to assess cardiovascular risk in primary prevention before initiating statin therapy. The other options are premature without risk assessment; a fasting profile is not necessary as non-fasting is acceptable for initial assessment.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (well-controlled on metformin) attends for a medication review. Her blood pressure is 138/86 mmHg, total cholesterol 5.6 mmol/L, LDL 3.5 mmol/L. She is a non-smoker. Her QRISK3 score is 21%. She is currently on no lipid-lowering therapy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to the lipid clinic for specialist advice
Advise lifestyle modification and recheck lipids in 3 months
questions.explanation: NICE (CG181) recommends offering atorvastatin 20mg for primary prevention in adults with a QRISK3 score ≥10%. Lifestyle advice should accompany but not delay pharmacotherapy; ezetimibe is second-line, and 40mg is not first-line initial dose.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known familial hypercholesterolaemia (on atorvastatin 40mg) presents to the Emergency Department with central chest pain of 2 hours’ duration. ECG shows ST-elevation in leads V2-V4. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give aspirin 300mg and load with ticagrelor
Call the interventional cardiology team for primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)
questions.explanation: For STEMI, primary PCI is the gold standard if available within 120 minutes. Aspirin is given but not the definitive action; thrombolysis is only if PCI not timely. Troponin is not needed to confirm what ECG already shows. The immediate priority is activating the cath lab.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with familial hypercholesterolaemia on simvastatin 40mg presents with bilateral thigh pain and dark urine. Creatine kinase (CK) is 4800 U/L (normal <200). Urinalysis is positive for blood but no red cells. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous hydration with normal saline
Refer to neurology for muscle biopsy
questions.explanation: CK >5 times upper limit of normal with symptoms indicates statin-induced myopathy; NICE advises to stop the statin immediately. Hydration may be needed for rhabdomyolysis but stopping the statin is the first step. Switching to another statin is not appropriate until resolution.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known familial hypercholesterolaemia (LDL 6.5 mmol/L) is planning pregnancy. She is currently on atorvastatin 40mg. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to the lipid clinic for consideration of LDL apheresis
Stop atorvastatin and advise to avoid pregnancy until off statin
Continue atorvastatin and monitor closely during pregnancy
questions.explanation: All statins are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity. NICE advises stopping statin at least 1-2 months before attempting conception. Pravastatin is not proven safe; ezetimibe is also not recommended. The immediate step is to stop the statin and provide contraceptive advice.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman attends a routine health check. She is asymptomatic. Her total cholesterol is 8.5 mmol/L. She has no known medical history. Which single clinical finding on examination would most strongly suggest a diagnosis of familial hypercholesterolaemia and prompt urgent specialist referral?
questions.explanation: Tendon xanthomata are pathognomonic for familial hypercholesterolaemia. Their presence in a patient with markedly elevated cholesterol indicates a high risk of premature cardiovascular disease and warrants specialist referral for cascade screening and aggressive lipid lowering.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. His serum appears lipaemic. Which single laboratory finding best explains his presentation and indicates a high risk of acute pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: NICE defines severe hypertriglyceridaemia as > 10 mmol/L, which is a well-established risk factor for acute pancreatitis. The clinical picture of epigastric pain with lipaemic serum is highly suggestive.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with known heterozygous familial hypercholesterolaemia (on atorvastatin 40 mg) presents to his GP with intermittent central chest pain on exertion. His ECG is normal. Which single feature from his history is a red flag that he may have significant coronary artery disease and requires urgent cardiology referral?
options: His father died suddenly at age 48
questions.explanation: A family history of premature sudden cardiac death in a first-degree relative is a major risk factor in FH, indicating very high cardiovascular risk. This together with anginal symptoms mandates urgent investigation for coronary artery disease.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known familial hypercholesterolaemia is currently 28 weeks pregnant. She had stopped her statin at conception. Her current lipid profile shows: total cholesterol 10.2 mmol/L, LDL 8.6 mmol/L, HDL 1.4 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.5 mmol/L. Which single measurement in her lipid profile is a red flag that merits consideration of LDL apheresis?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends referral for LDL apheresis in pregnant women with FH when LDL exceeds 8.5 mmol/L, due to high risk of acute pancreatitis and placental complications. Her LDL of 8.6 mmol/L crosses this threshold.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of myocardial infarction 3 years ago is on simvastatin 40 mg daily. He presents with new-onset generalised muscle aches, weakness, and dark urine. Which single laboratory finding would indicate a life-threatening complication requiring immediate hospital admission?
options: Creatine kinase > 5 times upper limit of normal
Aspartate transaminase > 3 times upper limit of normal
questions.explanation: NICE advises that a creatine kinase level > 5 times the upper limit of normal in the presence of muscle symptoms suggests rhabdomyolysis, a life-threatening statin adverse effect. Dark urine further raises suspicion, and urgent admission is needed to prevent acute kidney injury.
